LAW AND MOTION 302, PETITIONER EXPRESS MESSENGER SYSTEMS, INC., SUBCONTRACTING CONCEPTS (CT) LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE OR TAX MEMORANDUM OF COSTS. PETITIONERS' MOTION TO STRIKE OR TAX THE MEMORANDUM OF COSTS IS DENIED. WHILE THERE IS SOME CONFLICT IN THE RELEVANT AUTHORITIES, THE MORE RECENT CASE LAW ESTABLISHES THAT A PARTY WHO PREVAILS IN A LAWSUIT WHERE THE ONLY RELIEF SOUGHT IS A PETITION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS A PREVAILING PARTY FOR PURPOSES OF THE COSTS STATUTES, AT LEAST WITH RESPECT TO COSTS OTHER THAN ATTORNEY'S FEES. (SEE, E.G., OTAY RIVER CONSTRUCTORS V. SAN DIEGO EXPRESSWAY (2008)158 CAL. APP. 4TH 796). ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M.GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, TORREANOM@AOL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/HK)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Or Tax Memorandum Of Costs
Off Calendar Sep-25-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Joshua Elefant, Esq. 415-677-3193 of Littler Mendelson PC for Petitioner EXPRESS MESSENGER SYSTEMS, INC., SUBCONTRACTING CONCEPTS (CT) LLC.
Joseph Breall, Esq. 415-345-0545 of Breall & Breall for Respondents on 6 cases: Edvan Medeiros, Victr Mendez, Igor Gutman, Fabiano Neves,Elismar Moreira, Jose Valdivia.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court calls Lines 3 through 8 simultaneously. (515893, 515895, 515896, 515897, 515898, 515899) The Court discloses he knows Respondent's Counsel. However, it does not affect his ability to remain fair and impartial. The Court shares it's views re: ordinary costs. Petitioner argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER EXPRESS MESSENGER SYSTEMS, INC., SUBCONTRACTING CONCEPTS (CT) LLC's Motion To Strike Or Tax Memorandum Of Costs.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Petitioners' motion to strike or tax the memorandum of costs is denied. While there is some conflict in the relevant authorities, the more recent case law establishes that a party who prevails in a lawsuit where the only relief sought is a petition to compel arbitration is a prevailing party for purposes of the costs statutes, at least with respect to costs other than attorney's fees. (See, e.g., Otay River Constructors v. San Diego Expressway (2008)158 Cal. App. 4th 796).

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. =(302/HK)

Petition To Compel Arbitration And For Stay Of Dlse Proceedings
Off Calendar Jun-07-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Atty. Joshua E. Elefant of Littler Law Firm, Tel. No. 1-415-677-3193 appearing for the Petitioners;
Atty. Theresa J. Bischel, Dept. of Industrial Rel, Tel. No. 1-415-486-2083 appearing
for the Intervenor;
Atty. Joseph Breall of Breall & Breall, Tel. No. 1-415-345-0540 appearing for the Respondents.
RULING - Judge Kahn disclosed that Mr. Breall is the brother of his colleague, Susan Breall and Miss Bischel was counsel in 2 or more matters relating to a Berman hearing on a Petition to compel arbitration which he presided. Petitioners Express Messenger Systems, Inc. and Subcontracting Concepts CT, LLC,'s petition to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is denied. The arbitration agreement is unconscionable and severance of the substantively unconscionable provisions is not possible because the entire agreement is permeated with unconscionability. The New York choice of law provision is unconscionable and California law applies. (Pinela v Neiman Marcus Group (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 227, 246-247). The arbitration agreement is procedurally unconscionable. Subcontracting presented the adhesive contract in a take-it-or-leave-it manner and respondent Edvan Medeiros, whose English skills are limited, was asked to sign it "on the spot." (Medeiros Dec. pars. 1-7.) Paragraph 26 of the agreement refers to the American Arbitration Association, but the agreement does not clearly state what rules will govern the arbitration proceedings and Mr. Medeiros was never provided a copy of the governing rules. "The level of oppression is increased when, as here, the employer not only fails to provide a copy of the governing rules, but also fails to clearly identify which rules will govern so the employee could locate and review them." (Carbajal v. CWPSC, Inc. (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 227, 245). The arbitration agreement is substantively unconscionable. The agreement requires the parties to share the costs of arbitration and such costs are often prohibitively expensive and pose a barrier for an alleged employee such as Mr. Medeiros to vindicate his legal rights. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 108, 110-111). The agreement not only precludes Mr. Medeiros from taking advantage of the relatively inexpensive remedy of a hearing before the Labor Commissioner, it also bars representative and private attorney general claims. The arbitration agreement also bars the recovery of punitive damages, statutory penalties, equitable relief, and attorney's fees, which deprives Mr. Medeiros of statutory remedies. The arbitration agreement is permeated with unconscionability and thus severance of the unconscionable terms is not possible. Subcontracting's request to stay the DLSE claim is denied. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR # 8600 =302/hk
